title: The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco Names James D. Narron Product Manager of the National Cash Product Office - San Francisco Fed - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco Names James D. Narron Product Manager of the National Cash Product Office - San Francisco Fed Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: James D. NarronSan Francisco, CA — James D. Narron has been appointed product manager of the National Cash Product Office (CPO), the Bank announced today. Mr. Narron is currently senior vice president of the Fedwire Securities Services in the Federal Reserve Bank of New York’s wholesale product office. He was appointed to replace Mark Gould, who was promoted as the Bank’s first vice president and chief operating officer effective February 1, 2014. The CPO provides standard software and processing tools for all cash processing and vault automation systems nationwide, and guides policy, product development, and currency and coin capacity planning for all Federal Reserve cash operations. Prior to joining the New York Fed, he worked at the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, and the Board of Governors in Washington, D.C.

title: When Will the Fed End Its Zero Rate Policy? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: When Will the Fed End Its Zero Rate Policy? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Recent research on this episode tentatively suggests that investors moved earlier the date when they believed the Fed would exit its zero interest rate policy, even though Fed policymakers made few changes in their projections of appropriate monetary policy. The severe shock of the 2007–08 financial crisis prompted the Federal Reserve to quickly lower its target for its primary policy rate, the overnight federal funds rate, near to zero, where it has remained since. In December 2013, the FOMC added that, based on current projections, it expects to maintain the zero interest rate policy well past when the unemployment rate falls below 6½%. Furthermore, it is assumed that, in the normal state, investors consider the possibility of the policy rate reaching zero to be negligible. First, the shortest rate in the Treasury bond market is assumed to be constant at zero.

title: A bull’s-eye for Fed accountability - Econbrowser
description: A bull’s-eye for Fed accountability Econbrowser
summary: One of the sessions was on how central banks could better communicate their plans for using unconventional monetary policy. Suppose that our goal for monetary policy is to see an inflation rate around 2% and the unemployment rate close to 5.5%. With the inflation rate currently at 1.0% and the unemployment rate at 6.6%, that implies a value for the objective function of [math]V=(1.0-2.0)^{2}+(6.6-5.5)^{2}=2.2[/math]. This should be a principle for all effective monetary policy strategies and communications: to state monetary policy intentions clearly. But it’s also possible that we’re not going to see an unemployment rate much below 6%, no matter how hard the Fed tries.

title: Is It Still Worth Going to College? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Is It Still Worth Going to College? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Earnings outcomes by educational attainmentA common way to track the value of going to college is to estimate a college earnings premium, which is the amount college graduates earn relative to high school graduates. Using these data we compute the college earnings premium for three college graduate cohorts, namely those graduating in the 1950s–60s, the 1970s–80s, and the 1990s–2000s. Finally, it is worth noting that the benefits of college over high school also depend on employment, where college graduates also have an advantage. So, tuition amounts lower than our estimates make going to college strictly better in terms of earnings than not going to college. Moreover, once that investment is paid off, the extra income from the college earnings premium continues as a net gain to workers with a college degree.

title: Financial Market Outlook for Inflation - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Financial Market Outlook for Inflation Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: In this Economic Letter, we consider what we can learn about the outlook for inflation from financial market prices. While expectations of future inflation are priced into most financial assets, we focus on financial instruments with payoffs tied exclusively to inflation. The prices of these so-called inflation derivatives solely reflect financial market participants’ assessment of the inflation outlook. Figure 1Current outlook for CPI inflationFigure 1 shows the projection of future CPI inflation based on inflation swap rates, as of April 8, 2014. Based on this simple rule, a CPI inflation rate of 2.3% would be consistent with the FOMC’s inflation target.

summary: Deep inside the Federal Reserve Bank branch in Miami, beyond layers of security, teams of employees operate a warehouse handling millions of dollars in cash. description: The Economic Recovery and Monetary Policy: The Road Back to Ordinary Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Now, thankfully, the extraordinary is turning back into the ordinary, and we are starting down a path towards normalization, both for the economy and monetary policy. title: Does Slower Growth Imply Lower Interest Rates? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Does Slower Growth Imply Lower Interest Rates? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Moreover, a decline in the natural rate of interest would tend to lower every other real and nominal interest rate inthe economy. Therefore, understanding the linkage between economic growth and the natural rate is crucial forforecasting all types of interest rates. Although forecasts of potential growth orthe natural rate are rarely reported, we can construct reasonable proxies from long-run forecasts of GDP growth, theshort-term interest rate, and inflation. Because we do not directly observe the natural rate of interest or potential trend growth, we construct proxies fromlong-run forecasts of real GDP growth and short-term real interest rates. For instance, Goldman Sachs (2014) recently examined the effect of real per-capita GDP growth onshort-term real interest rates in 20 countries since the early 1800s.

description: Mixed Signals: Labor Markets and Monetary Policy Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: In this Economic Letter we discuss what several measures of labor market slack tell us about conditions in the labor market and the implications for monetary policy. Assessing labor market slackTable 1Definitions of measures of labor market slackThe Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) publishes several measures that can be used to evaluate labor market slack, or the degree to which labor resources are being underutilized (see Table 1). Conflicting policy prescriptionsOne way to see how conflicting signals from the labor market affect monetary policy is by applying a simple policy rule. This view is consistent with the relatively quick recovery of the short-term unemployment rate relative to the long-term unemployment rate. “Labor Market Dynamics and Monetary Policy.” Speech at the FRB Kansas City Economic Symposium, Jackson Hole, WY, August 22.

title: Top Fed Policy Group Brought Up Leak to Private Newsletter - propublica.org
description: Top Fed Policy Group Brought Up Leak to Private Newsletter propublica.org
summary: HTMLTop Fed Policy Group Brought Up Leak to Private NewsletterA leak of potentially market moving information from the Federal Reserve became a topic of conversation in the high-level Fed group that sets monetary policy. The group’s deliberations take place in private, but the Fed routinely releases a summary three weeks later. In this case, the newsletter containing the inside information was available only to Medley’s private clients, who in theory could have traded on it. Past leaks have prompted the chairman of the Federal Reserve to call in the inspector general or other outside investigative agencies. Related stories: Read more of reporter Jake Bernstein’s coverage of the Federal Reserve.

summary: Since population aging is widespread across the world’s largest countries, this raises the question of whether global aging could adversely affect the U.S. equity market even further. Global demographics and equity valuesIn theory, the rapid aging of the global population is likely to have additional adverse implications for U.S. stock values. The solid lines denote the estimated relation between the P/E ratio and M/O ratio.

description: Persistent Overoptimism about Economic Growth Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: FRBSF Economic Letter 2015-03 | February 2, 2015Since 2007, Federal Open Market Committee participants have been persistently too optimistic about future U.S. economic growth. Real GDP growth forecasts have typically started high, but then are revised down over time as the incoming data continue to disappoint. The SEP growth forecasts have typically started high, but then are revised down over time as the incoming data continue to disappoint. Growth forecasts revised downFigure 1 plots the evolution of the SEP’s midpoint growth forecast for the years 2008 through 2010. The overoptimistic SEP growth forecasts for 2011 through 2013 were eventually cut in half, each ending around 2%.

title: The FR 2420 Data Collection: A New Base for the Fed Funds Rate - Liberty Street Economics
description: The FR 2420 Data Collection: A New Base for the Fed Funds Rate Liberty Street Economics
summary: These new data, collected on form FR 2420, have helped the Fed better understand activity in the fed funds and Eurodollar markets. We plan to publish other posts on the fed funds and Eurodollar markets over the next several months. Given these benefits, the Fed plans to use FR 2420 data to eventually replace brokered data in calculating the FFER. As in the brokered data, the majority of volume in the FR 2420 data occurs close to the brokered FFER. And, since July 2014, when the New York Fed started collecting FR 2420 data, the daily volume-weighted average rate calculated from FR 2420 data has differed from the FFER very little, by less than 1 basis point on average.

description: Interest Rates and House Prices: Pill or Poison? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Higher interest rates make mortgages more expensive and could prevent borrowers from bidding up house prices to create a boom. This Economic Letter investigates the link between interest rates, mortgage lending, and house prices. An experiment based on international dataHow successfully interest rates can curb an asset price boom depends on how responsive housing demand is to interest rates. Mortgage lending and house prices are sensitive to interest ratesIn recent research (Jordà, Schularick, and Taylor 2015) we quantify the responsiveness of mortgage lending and house prices to interest rates using statistical methods that exploit the trilemma of international finance. Our results are consistent with findings in the literature: Long-term rates do not respond one-to-one to fluctuations in short-term rates because short-term rates typically revert to the average more quickly.

summary: The Sun Yefang Prize in Economic Science rewards outstanding contributions to economic research about China. The prize is considered the highest ranked award for economics in China, and has been awarded biennially by the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences since 1984. The Center promotes cooperation among central banks in the Pacific Basin and provides insight into and analysis of economic policy issues affecting the region.

title: Fed Communication and the Zero Lower Bound - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Fed Communication and the Zero Lower Bound Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: From 2008 through late 2015, the Federal Reserve kept its policy rate, the federal funds rate, at essentially its lower bound near zero. We find that the zero bound altered but did not substantially constrain monetary policy, as Fed communications continued to affect long-term interest rates. Figure 2Estimated yield changes following Fed communication surprises Note: Yield response by Treasury maturity to a one-standard- deviation Fed communication surprise, measured as hundredths of a percentage point (basis points). To assess whether the zero bound constrained the monetary authority, this Letter compares the effects of Fed communications before and after the federal funds rate target reached its zero lower bound. “Monetary Policy at the Zero Lower Bound: Putting Theory into Practice.” Working paper, Hutchins Center on Fiscal & Monetary Policy at Brookings.

title: Monetary Policy in a Low R-star World - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Monetary Policy in a Low R-star World Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: In the post-financial crisis world, however, new realities pose significant challenges for the conduct of monetary policy. We have already gotten a first taste of the effects of a low r-star, with uncomfortably low inflation and growth despite very low interest rates. Finally, monetary policy frameworks should be critically reevaluated to identify potential improvements in the context of a low r-star. Although targeting a low inflation rate generally has been successful at taming inflation in the past, it is not as well-suited for a low r-star era. “Three Lessons for Monetary Policy in a Low Inflation Era.” Journal of Money, Credit, and Banking 32(4, November), pp.

title: Clearing the Fog: The Effects of Weather on Jobs - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Clearing the Fog: The Effects of Weather on Jobs Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: We then plug real-time weather data by county and month into the model and add up the county-level effects to estimate monthly weather effects on national employment. Applying this to employment changes over recent months shows that weather effects were especially important this past spring. Estimating local weather effectsWilson (2016) estimates weather’s economic effects using regression analysis, a statistical technique common in economics. In contrast, weather effects during the spring were quite large according to the estimates using the county model. This suggests that empirical models of weather effects can be useful to give policymakers a cleaner read on the economy’s underlying strength.

title: How Tight is Michigan’s Labor Market? - Chicago - Federal Reserve Bank
description: How Tight is Michigan’s Labor Market? Chicago - Federal Reserve Bank
summary: As the Layoffs and Other Separations Rate increases, it puts upward pressure on the unemployment rate. The Chicago Fed Hiring Rate for Unemployed Workers is an estimate of the percentage of previously unemployed workers that transitioned into employment (or out of the labor force) during the BLS reference week. When unemployed workers successfully find a job (or leave the labor force), it reduces the upward pressure on the unemployment rate coming from layoffs and other job separations. As such, an increase in the Chicago Fed Hiring Rate for Unemployed Workers puts downward pressure on the unemployment rate. Second, our measures are constructed from aggregates and scaled in order to preserve a direct relationship with the BLS unemployment rate.

summary: San Francisco, California — The Board of Directors of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco has appointed Mary C. Daly executive vice president and director of Economic Research, the Bank announced today. She succeeds Glenn D. Rudebusch, who will remain at the Bank as executive vice president and senior policy advisor. In announcing the appointment, John C. Williams, president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco said, “Mary has become one of the most respected economists in the Federal Reserve System over her 20-year tenure at the San Francisco Fed. title: The Federal Reserve's Response to the Financial Crisis - Federal Reserve Board (.gov)
description: The Federal Reserve's Response to the Financial Crisis Federal Reserve Board (.gov)
summary: The twelve Federal Reserve Banks provide banking services to depository institutions and the federal government. The Federal Reserve Banks have been providing payment services to the banking industry since shortly after the Federal Reserve System was established in 1913. The MCA subjected all depository institutions, not just member banks, to reserve requirements and also gave all depository institutions access to the Federal Reserve's payment services. The pricing of Federal Reserve services has facilitated competition between the Federal Reserve Banks and private-sector providers of payment services, which has resulted in a more efficient payment system. Since 1980, the Federal Reserve has adopted policies regarding the provision of Reserve Bank payment services.

title: How the Federal Reserve controls interest rates - Econbrowser
description: How the Federal Reserve controls interest rates Econbrowser
summary: The way in which the Federal Reserve controls the short-term interest rate today is completely different from the way things worked ten years ago. The second interest rate that the ECB chooses is the interest rate it pays to banks on funds left in their account with the ECB overnight. The second policy rate is an interest rate that the Fed will pay on reverse repo operations. This is why the effective fed funds rate drops precipitously on those days, getting much closer to the effective floor posed by the reverse repo rate. How the Federal Reserve controls the fed funds rate today is thus a fairly complicated story.

description: El-Erian, Minerd expect Federal Reserve to remain active SmartBrief
summary: According to some of the leading minds at the Milken Institute Global Conference, the Federal Reserve will continue to play an active role in the US economy throughout the rest of 2017 and beyond. Speaking on a panel tasked with assessing Global Capital Markets, Mohamed El-Erian, the chief economic advisor at Allianz, said he expects the Fed to finish the year with a total of 3 rate hikes. El-Erian, who is known for his not-so-rosy economic prognostication, drew laughs from the crowd when he explained, “I am not usually optimistic.” But nevertheless, he said the fundamentals are there for such activity from the Fed. title: Demographic Transition and Low U.S. Interest Rates - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Demographic Transition and Low U.S. Interest Rates Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: One possible explanation is the dramatic worldwide demographic transition, with people living longer and population growth rates declining. This demographic transition in the United States—particularly the steady increase in life expectancy—put significant downward pressure on interest rates between 1990 and 2016. Because demographic movements tend to be long-lasting, their ongoing effects could keep interest rates near the lower bound longer. Figure 1 reports median real interest rates across some developed economies and shows that, in fact, real interest rates have been trending down for more than two decades. “The Low Level of Global Interest Rates.” Speech at the Conference to Celebrate Arminio Fraga’s 60 Years, Casa das Garças, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, July 31.

title: Kevin Warsh Should Not Be Federal Reserve Chairman - Niskanen Center
description: Kevin Warsh Should Not Be Federal Reserve Chairman Niskanen Center
summary: The economy’s true ills were structural, Warsh argued. Contrary to Warsh’s warning, the Federal Reserve went ahead with its plan. Today the unemployment rate is at 4.3 percent, and inflation is still below the Federal Reserve’s target of 2 percent. By 2013, the Eurozone unemployment rate had climbed to 12 percent, while the U.S. was at 8 percent and slowly but steadily on the path to recovery. We will need a Federal Reserve Chair who has the judgment to know which levers to pull, and a commitment to aggressively pursue the Fed’s legal mandates.

summary: Note: This analysis provides insight into how the fed funds market might react to changes in the aggregate level of bank reserves. Modelling the Fed Funds MarketWe formalize our argument within the context of a simple model that captures several of the key features of the fed funds market before and after the expansion in aggregate reserves. The diagram below illustrates key features of the fed funds market, along with the policy rates that the Fed sets. In one scenario, aggregate reserves decrease entirely through a reduction in bank B’s balances: Average bank reserves could be as low as $10 per bank before bank B runs out of reserves and starts demanding funds—a drop of more than 80 percent in total reserves. title: Information in the Yield Curve about Future Recessions - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Information in the Yield Curve about Future Recessions Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: FRBSF Economic Letter 2018-20 | August 27, 2018The ability of the Treasury yield curve to predict future recessions has recently received a great deal of public attention. It measures the steepness of the yield curve, the graph of interest rates across all available maturities, with a positive term spread corresponding to an upward sloping yield curve. Our results in Figure 2 reveal that this adjusted term spread is a less accurate predictor of recessions than the observed unadjusted term spread. On the one hand, yield curve inversions could cause future recessions because short-term rates are elevated and tight monetary policy is slowing down the economy. “(Don’t Fear) the Yield Curve.” FEDS Notes, Federal Reserve Board of Governors, June 28.

description: Sylvain Leduc Appointed Executive Vice President and Director of Research at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco - San Francisco Fed Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: San Francisco, California — The Board of Directors of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco appointed Sylvain Leduc executive vice president and director of Economic Research, the Bank announced today. He brings more than 20 years of experience working within the Federal Reserve System and as a Deputy Governor of the Bank of Canada to this role. From 1998 to 2008, Leduc also served as a senior economist in the Research Department of the Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia. Leduc returned to the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco in August 2018 as associate director of Research. With his appointment to director of Research, Leduc also becomes a member of the Bank’s Executive Committee, the governance body responsible for the strategic direction of the organization.

title: A Review of the Fed’s Unconventional Monetary Policy - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: A Review of the Fed’s Unconventional Monetary Policy Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: The federal funds rate is the short-term interest rate that is our main conventional monetary policy tool. Forward Policy GuidanceThe second main way the Fed has affected longer-term interest rates and the economy is through forward policy guidance. After each monetary policy meeting, the FOMC releases a statement describing the current state of the economy, monetary policy, and the outlook for both. Thus, the Federal Reserve’s forward policy guidance can affect longer-term interest rates by changing people’s expectations about the future path of monetary policy. Looking to what will constitute the new normal for future monetary policy, one must weigh the costs and benefits of conventional and unconventional policy tools in different circumstances.

title: Why Is the Fed’s Balance Sheet Still So Big? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Why Is the Fed’s Balance Sheet Still So Big? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: This Economic Letter discusses this choice and other factors underlying the increase in the Fed’s balance sheet since the Great Recession. The balance sheetLike any other financial institution, the Fed has a balance sheet with two sides: assets and liabilities. Before the crisis, the Fed’s balance sheet was roughly $870 billion dollars, or about 7% of GDP. As a result, the U.S. Treasury’s deposits are a liability on the Fed’s balance sheet. ConclusionThe size of the Fed’s balance sheet will not return to its pre-crisis level.

summary: Since July 2019, the FOMC has lowered the target range for the FFR twice, effectively injecting liquidity into the banking system. But ahead of that meeting, the effective FFR spiked, exceeding the upper limit of the target range. On September 17, 2019, the Federal Reserve Bank of New York began conducting temporary open market operations through overnight repurchase agreements: That is, it purchased Treasury securities held by banks. The FRED graph above shows these recent operations.

title: ICYMI: In CNBC Op-Ed, Pressley & Stein Ask: Do Black Lives Matter to the Fed? - House.gov
description: ICYMI: In CNBC Op-Ed, Pressley & Stein Ask: Do Black Lives Matter to the Fed? House.gov
summary: WASHINGTON – Today, in an op-ed published on CNBC.com, Congresswoman Ayanna Pressley (MA-07) and Author and UCLA Historian David Stein called on the Federal Reserve to take specific steps to address Black unemployment, which has historically exceeded the overall unemployment rate in America. The Federal Reserve is no exception. First, the Federal Reserve must expand its municipal liquid facility to ensure that big corporations aren’t getting a better deal than our communities. As Congresswoman Rashida Tlaib noted to Powell, just as Congress must do more to ease state and municipal budget shortfalls, so too must the Federal Reserve. Finally, the Federal Reserve must not stand in the way of the only true path to maximum employment—a federal jobs guarantee.

summary: The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday released annual audited financial statements for the Federal Reserve System for 2025. The Board released audited individual and combined statements for the 12 Federal Reserve Banks, the Board, and one limited liability company (LLC), relating to the Board's response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The audited financial statements provide information about assets, liabilities, and operations as of December 31, 2025. The Federal Reserve Act requires the Board to order an annual independent audit of the financial statements of each Reserve Bank and the Board. In addition to annual audited financial statements, the Board also publishes a weekly comprehensive balance sheet and quarterly financial reports.

title: Indian American Naureen Hassan Appointed First VP and COO of Federal Reserve Bank of New York - American Kahani
description: Indian American Naureen Hassan Appointed First VP and COO of Federal Reserve Bank of New York American Kahani
summary: Financial services veteran Naureen Hassan has been appointed first vice president and chief operating officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, effective March 15. As first vice president, the Indian American will be the New York Fed’s second ranking officer as well as an alternate voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee. Hassan has served in various capacities in the financial services industry for nearly 20 years, focusing primarily on digital and business process transformation. Most recently, Hassan was the Chief Digital Officer for Morgan Stanley Wealth Management (MSWM) and a member of the MSWM Operating Committee. Previously, Hassan was the executive vice president leading Investor Services strategy, segments and platforms at the Charles Schwab Corporation.

summary: The FOMC said it would maintain its monthly purchases of securities to pressure long-term interest rates lower, facilitating investment. It’s all part of an effort to keep long-term rates down …Long-term rates contain expectations for short-term rates and the risks of inflation or deflation. The gradual decline in long-term interest rates was possible only when inflation was squeezed out the economy.

title: Federal Reserve Releases CECL SCALE Model - CBIZ
description: Federal Reserve Releases CECL SCALE Model CBIZ
summary: Last month brought good news for small community banks: The Federal Reserve Bank rolled out its Scaled CECL Allowance for Losses Estimator (SCALE model), which is intended to simplify the process of calculating current expected credit losses (CECL) and the resulting allowance. Included in the FASB Accounting Standards Update 2016-13, current expected credit losses (CECL) became effective for…small public businesses and all non-public businesses. The SCALE model is an Excel-based model that includes the required components of CECL, including the use of an historical loss rate, current economic condition factors, and forecasted future economic factors. Financial institutions should access the information and resources provided by the Federal Reserve to determine if the SCALE model might work for them. For additional information on CECL and the SCALE model, please contact CBIZ’s Financial I

title: At the New York Fed: Implications of Federal Reserve Actions in Response to the COVID-19 Pandemic - Liberty Street Economics
description: At the New York Fed: Implications of Federal Reserve Actions in Response to the COVID-19 Pandemic Liberty Street Economics
summary: Nina Boyarchenko, Anna Kovner, and Antoine MartinOn September 30 and October 1, 2021, the New York Fed held a virtual conference on the implications of the Fed’s actions in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. Andy Haughwout, David Arseneau, and Or Shachar – all economists in the Federal Reserve System — presented research on the design and impact of the Fed’s interventions. title: Why Is U.S. Inflation Higher than in Other Countries? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Why Is U.S. Inflation Higher than in Other Countries? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: However, since the first half of 2021, U.S. inflation has increasingly outpaced inflation in other developed countries. Though many of the pandemic distortions are common to other countries, we show that U.S. inflation has risen more quickly and increasingly diverged from inflation in other OECD (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development) countries. A tighter range indicates that most OECD countries in our sample experienced inflation rates similar to each other. The small difference between U.S. and OECD inflation during this period is well known as many of the OECD countries struggled to get inflation up to target following the Global Financial Crisis and subsequent euro-area sovereign debt crisis. That is, inflation reflects a combination of the public’s views on future inflation, inflation inertia, and how hot the economy is running.

summary: SAN FRANCISCO – The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco announced today that Louise Willard has been appointed to the position of chief information officer and executive vice president of Information and Technology Services, effective July 15, 2022. Louise WillardWillard, who has worked at the San Francisco Fed since 1997, previously served as the Bank’s senior vice president and CIO of the Cash Product Office. She succeeds Gopa Kumar, the Bank’s previous CIO, who retired earlier this year after a 28-year career at the SF Fed. “I am honored and excited to lead the SF Fed’s technology strategy,” said Willard. To learn more about Information and Technology Services jobs at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, go to ITS jobs at the SF Fed.

title: How the Federal Reserve’s Monetary Policy Drives Housing Inequality - Shelterforce
description: How the Federal Reserve’s Monetary Policy Drives Housing Inequality Shelterforce
summary: Most surprising is the lack of attention on the Federal Reserve, even though its policy interventions have had profound effects on housing prices. By 2018, housing prices had recovered from their Great Recession losses and recommenced their inexorable increase. That, however, was not about the red-hot housing market, but rather due to its concern about persistent and growing inflation. Housing prices have soared globally as well. In the U.S., President Joe Biden does not seem to be too concerned about the supercharged housing market and its role in worsening inequality.

title: Remote Work and Housing Demand - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Remote Work and Housing Demand Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: More remote work leads to higher house prices and rentsFollowing the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic, remote work increased dramatically across the entire country. However, remote work varies substantially across cities, which can help us understand the effect remote work has had on housing markets. The figure shows that CBSAs with more remote work before the pandemic saw larger increases in remote work during the pandemic. However, by late 2020, the CBSAs with higher shares of remote work had significantly higher house price growth than those with less remote work. Thus, pandemic remote work that was caused by the preexisting share of work that was remote can be used to estimate the causal effect of remote work on pandemic house price growth.

title: How Do Deposit Rates Respond to Monetary Policy? - Liberty Street Economics
description: How Do Deposit Rates Respond to Monetary Policy? Liberty Street Economics
summary: Understanding how deposit terms adapt to changes in policy rates is important to understanding the impact of monetary policy more broadly. In this post, we evaluate the pass through of the fed funds rate to deposit rates (that is, deposit betas) over the past several interest rate cycles and discuss factors that affect deposit rates. Deposit rates follow the fed funds rate but are typically lower, particularly when the fed funds rate is elevated. Deposit Gap, Deposit Rates and the Fed Funds RateSources: FR Y-9C; H.15; and authors’ calculations. title: When the Fed Raises Rates, Are Banks Less Profitable? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: When the Fed Raises Rates, Are Banks Less Profitable? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: With long-term fixed rate assets and short-term liabilities that have flexible rates, banks may be strongly exposed to interest rate risk. On the liability side, banks’ funding costs do not necessarily move one-for-one with changes in short-term rates, which means interest rates that banks pay on deposits are inherently rigid. For instance, while variable-rate loans and rigid deposit rates reduce their exposure, banks’ net interest margins still decline following unexpected increases in short-term policy rates, though this pass-through is muted. The income gap therefore approximates the extent to which a bank’s net interest income is sensitive to changes in interest rates. Deposit flows and asset reallocations within the financial systemHow do depositors react when policy rates move up and deposit rates are held down?

title: The Role of Immigration in U.S. Labor Market Tightness - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: The Role of Immigration in U.S. Labor Market Tightness Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: I assess the labor market using the vacancy-to-unemployment (V–U) ratio, which is a well-known measure of the degree of labor market tightness, with a higher V–U ratio indicating a tighter labor market (Barnichon and Shapiro 2022). Labor market tightness and its contributing factorsOne way to measure the strength of the labor market is to compare the number of vacant positions employers would like to fill to the number of people looking for jobs. Role of immigration in population and labor force growthImmigrants contribute to the U.S. economy through the supply of labor as well as through entrepreneurship. However, the primary way recent entrants affect the U.S. labor market is by increasing labor supply, since they are more likely to work than demand labor services. The foreign-born labor force grew rapidly in 2022, closing the labor force gap created by the pandemic.

title: A Directorship at a Federal Reserve Bank is Good News for Banks, but May be Bad News for the Fed - promarket.org
description: A Directorship at a Federal Reserve Bank is Good News for Banks, but May be Bad News for the Fed promarket.org
summary: Far before the collapse of SVB, I provided systematic evidence that banks appear to benefit from their directorships on Federal Reserve Banks. In my (2017) paper “Good News for Some Banks”, I examine whether banks with Reserve Bank directorships appear to benefit from their positions. Figure 1: The Structure of Federal Reserve Bank BoardsFigure 1 shows how Federal Reserve Bank boards are structured after the Dodd-Frank Act. Senator Dodd’s 2009 reform bill proposed to strip banks of their power to select Federal Reserve Bank directors. On March 23, 2023 Senator Sanders again introduced legislation to prevent big bank executives from serving on Federal Reserve Bank boards.

summary: The Federal Reserve on Thursday announced that its new system for instant payments, the FedNow® Service, is now live. Banks and credit unions of all sizes can sign up and use this tool to instantly transfer money for their customers, any time of the day, on any day of the year. “The Federal Reserve built the FedNow Service to help make everyday payments over the coming years faster and more convenient,” said Federal Reserve Chair Jerome H. description: Federal Reserve Board announces preliminary financial information for the Federal Reserve Banks’ income and expenses in 2023 federalreserve.gov
summary: The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday released annual audited financial statements for the Federal Reserve System for 2025. The Board released audited individual and combined statements for the 12 Federal Reserve Banks, the Board, and one limited liability company (LLC), relating to the Board's response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The audited financial statements provide information about assets, liabilities, and operations as of December 31, 2025. The Federal Reserve Act requires the Board to order an annual independent audit of the financial statements of each Reserve Bank and the Board. In addition to annual audited financial statements, the Board also publishes a weekly comprehensive balance sheet and quarterly financial reports.

title: You Decide: Will the Fed Be Involved in Politics in 2024? - NC State University
description: You Decide: Will the Fed Be Involved in Politics in 2024? NC State University
summary: If the Fed aggressively cuts its key interest rate, interest rates like mortgage rates, credit card rates and other borrowing rates will likely follow. Conversely, if the Fed doesn’t reduce interest rates, or if it reduces rates very slowly, the opposite sentiments could emerge. Lowering of interest rates continued into 1992, but significantly slowed in the months immediately prior to the November election. Yet throughout Nixon’s first term from 1969 to 1973, the Fed cut interest rates by almost half. But it will be impossible to ignore the political implications of the Fed’s interest rate decisions.

title: Rise in investor-owned single-family rentals prompts policy responses - Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis
description: Rise in investor-owned single-family rentals prompts policy responses Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis
summary: Using the Minneapolis Fed’s capacity for applied research, data exploration, and policy analysis, Libby Starling focuses on deepening the Bank’s understanding of housing affordability, concentrating on effective housing policies and practices that make a difference for low- and moderate-income families in the Ninth Federal Reserve District. Prior to joining the Fed in 2019, Starling was the deputy director of the Community Development Division at the Metropolitan Council, where, among other accomplishments, she led the agency’s development of its 2040 Housing Policy Plan. Previously, she was the director of research and evaluation for the Minnesota Housing Finance Agency. She holds a Master of Planning degree from the Humphrey School of Public Affairs at the University of Minnesota.

title: How Quickly Do Prices Respond to Monetary Policy? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: How Quickly Do Prices Respond to Monetary Policy? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Many researchers have estimated the speed and strength of the economy’s response to monetary policy, notably Romer and Romer (2004). However, not all prices of the component goods and services react to monetary policy in the same way. We find substantial variation in how prices react to monetary policy, which suggests that understanding the makeup of overall inflation can provide insights into the transmission of monetary policy to inflation. Measuring how prices react to monetary policyTo understand which goods and services are most responsive to monetary policy, we need to determine how their prices react to changes in the federal funds rate, the Federal Reserve’s main policy rate. This is consistent with the view that the full effects of monetary policy tightening have yet to be felt.

title: Why the Federal Reserve will wait until September to cut interest rates, expert explains - Northeastern Global News
description: Why the Federal Reserve will wait until September to cut interest rates, expert explains Northeastern Global News
summary: Why the Federal Reserve will wait until September to cut interest rates, expert explainsWith inflation decreasing, the time to cut interest rates is near, Northeastern economist Bob Triest says. Photo by Michael Kuenne/PRESSCOV/Sipa via AP ImagesWall Street’s eyes will be on the Federal Reserve this week, as the board meets Wednesday to decide whether to cut interest rates. Northeastern University economist Bob Triest says there are two reasons why the Fed will likely wait to cut rates until its next meeting in September. Recent inflation peaked at 9.1% in June 2022, and the Federal Reserve has raised interest rates aggressively in an attempt to bring inflation down to 2%. Second will be hints at a rate cut in September, the kind of prepping of the market that usually precedes a policy change.

summary: Almost 19 percent of the firms reported increases in new orders, 23 percent reported decreases, and 35 percent reported no change. Almost 34 percent of the responding firms reported increases in sales/revenues, while 21 percent reported decreases. Firms Report Overall Decreases in EmploymentThe full-time employment index fell 22 points to -11.3 this month, its lowest reading since August 2024. Almost 44 percent of the respondents reported higher input prices, 36 percent reported no change, and 4 percent reported decreases. Almost 18 percent of the firms reported increases in prices received, and 8 percent reported decreases.

title: Large Bank Credit Card and Mortgage Data 2024 Q3 Narrative - Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank
description: Large Bank Credit Card and Mortgage Data 2024 Q3 Narrative Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank
summary: 2025 Q4 Insights ReportPublished: April 8, 2026The fourth quarter of 2025 marked modest improvements in credit performance for credit card accounts, with early-stage delinquency declining from a series high two years earlier. That said, credit card performance remains stressed relative to pre-pandemic norms. Furthermore, credit card interest rates are historically high, a considerable burden for cardholders who carry credit card debt. Credit card originations rose in the fourth quarter, and large banks were more willing to offer card services to less creditworthy consumers, with slightly looser application standards. First-lien mortgage credit performance remains strong, with delinquencies steady near series lows.

title: Introducing the Chicago Fed Labor Market Indicators - Chicago - Federal Reserve Bank
description: Introducing the Chicago Fed Labor Market Indicators Chicago - Federal Reserve Bank
summary: As the Layoffs and Other Separations Rate increases, it puts upward pressure on the unemployment rate. The Chicago Fed Hiring Rate for Unemployed Workers is an estimate of the percentage of previously unemployed workers that transitioned into employment (or out of the labor force) during the BLS reference week. When unemployed workers successfully find a job (or leave the labor force), it reduces the upward pressure on the unemployment rate coming from layoffs and other job separations. As such, an increase in the Chicago Fed Hiring Rate for Unemployed Workers puts downward pressure on the unemployment rate. Second, our measures are constructed from aggregates and scaled in order to preserve a direct relationship with the BLS unemployment rate.

title: What Is a Tariff Shock? Insights from 150 years of Tariff Policy - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: What Is a Tariff Shock? Insights from 150 years of Tariff Policy Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: 2025-26 | November 13, 2025In this paper we exploit 150 years of tariff policy in the US and abroad to estimate the short-run effects of tariff shocks on macro aggregates. Exploiting this quasi-random nature of tariff variations, we find that a tariff hike raises unemployment (lowers economic activity) and lowers inflation. Using only tariff changes driven by long-run considerations—a traditional narrative identification—gives similar results. These findings point towards tariff shocks acting through an aggregate demand channel. Insights from 150 years of Tariff Policy.” Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco Working Paper 2025-26. https://doi.org/10.24148/wp2025-26
